comment_text.sn5.10:1.2: Although these verses are much-quoted in Buddhist literature, Vajirā is only known from this Sutta.
comment_text.sn5.10:2.1: The challenge is the same as the previous, with “sentient being” for “puppet”.
comment_text.sn5.10:5.4: Vajirā directly opposes the Upaniṣadic view that the Self underlying the diversity of experience is no mere convention, but the true reality masked by appearances. Compare Bṛhadāraṇyaka Upaniṣad 2.5.15 and Chāndogya Upaniṣad 7.15.1, where all beings are fixed in Divinity like spokes in the hub of a wheel. The Upaniṣads say that the Self or the mind is the charioteer and the body the chariot (Katha Upaniṣad 1.3.3–9, Maitrī Upaniṣad 2.6, Paiṅgala Upaniṣad 4.1).

root_text.sn5.10:1.2: Atha kho vajirā bhikkhunī pubbaṇhasamayaṁ nivāsetvā pattacīvaramādāya sāvatthiṁ piṇḍāya pāvisi.
root_text.sn5.10:1.3: Sāvatthiyaṁ piṇḍāya caritvā pacchābhattaṁ piṇḍapātapaṭikkantā yena andhavanaṁ tenupasaṅkami divāvihārāya.
root_text.sn5.10:1.4: Andhavanaṁ ajjhogāhetvā aññatarasmiṁ rukkhamūle divāvihāraṁ nisīdi.
root_text.sn5.10:1.5: Atha kho māro pāpimā vajirāya bhikkhuniyā bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo samādhimhā cāvetukāmo yena vajirā bhikkhunī tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā vajiraṁ bhikkhuniṁ gāthāya ajjhabhāsi:
root_text.sn5.10:3.2: “ko nu khvāyaṁ manusso vā amanusso vā gāthaṁ bhāsatī”ti?
root_text.sn5.10:3.4: “māro kho ayaṁ pāpimā mama bhayaṁ chambhitattaṁ lomahaṁsaṁ uppādetukāmo samādhimhā cāvetukāmo gāthaṁ bhāsatī”ti.
root_text.sn5.10:3.5: Atha kho vajirā bhikkhunī “māro ayaṁ pāpimā” iti viditvā, māraṁ pāpimantaṁ gāthāhi paccabhāsi:
root_text.sn5.10:7.1: Atha kho māro pāpimā “jānāti maṁ vajirā bhikkhunī”ti dukkhī dummano tatthevantaradhāyīti.

translation_text.sn5.10:1.2: Then the nun Vajirā robed up in the morning and, taking her bowl and robe, entered Sāvatthī for alms.
translation_text.sn5.10:1.3: She wandered for alms in Sāvatthī. After the meal, on her return from almsround, she went to the Dark Forest for the day’s meditation,
translation_text.sn5.10:1.4: plunged deep into it, and sat at the root of a tree to meditate.
translation_text.sn5.10:1.5: Then Māra the Wicked, wanting to make the nun Vajirā feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make her fall away from immersion, went up to her and addressed her in verse:
translation_text.sn5.10:3.2: “Who’s speaking this verse, a human or a non-human?”
translation_text.sn5.10:3.4: “This is Māra the Wicked, wanting to make me feel fear, terror, and goosebumps, wanting to make me fall away from immersion!”
translation_text.sn5.10:3.5: Then Vajirā, knowing that this was Māra the Wicked, replied to him in verse:
translation_text.sn5.10:4.1: “Why do you believe there’s such a thing as a <j>‘sentient being’?
translation_text.sn5.10:4.3: This is just a pile of conditions,
translation_text.sn5.10:4.4: you won’t find a sentient being here.
translation_text.sn5.10:5.3: So too, when the aggregates are present
translation_text.sn5.10:5.4: ‘sentient being’ is the convention we use.
translation_text.sn5.10:6.1: But it’s only suffering that comes to be,
translation_text.sn5.10:6.3: Naught but suffering comes to be,
translation_text.sn5.10:7.1: Then Māra the Wicked, thinking, “The nun Vajirā knows me!” miserable and sad, vanished right there.
translation_text.sn5.10:8.6: The Linked Discourses on Nuns are completed.
